# CaveWhere User Manual

CaveWhere is a Qt/C++ desktop app for cave mapping and 3D visualization: it
morphs hand-drawn 2D survey sketches into 3D cave models, detects loop-closure
blunders, syncs projects between team members over Git, and imports LiDAR notes.
This is the machine-readable index of the user manual — read the linked pages to
answer user questions. Each entry is `- [Title](path): summary`.

This manual is being built out; pages are added chapter by chapter. Every page
here exists and is safe to read. See AUTHORING.md for the conventions all pages
follow.

## Start here

- [Why CaveWhere](concepts/why-cavewhere.md): The problems CaveWhere solves for cave surveyors (2D→3D morphing, loop closure, Git sync, LiDAR, keywords, project format) and how features map to them.
- [Glossary](concepts/glossary.md): Definitions of cave-surveying and CaveWhere terms — station, shot, LRUD, scrap, declination, grid convergence, loop closure, backsight, trip, lead, morphing.

## Chapters

- [The 3D View](view-3d/the-3d-view.md): Navigate the 3D cave model — orbit/pan/zoom (turntable), aim the camera by exact angle (azimuth, plan/profile vertical angle, orthogonal/perspective projection), read the compass and scale bar, and filter the scene with keyword layers.
- [Add Notes to a Trip](notes/add-a-note.md): Import the drawings made underground as notes on a trip, however they were recorded — scanned paper, a PDF, a digital sketch exported as SVG from an app like TopoDroid, or a phone LiDAR scan; they all import the same way. Covers Add → Notes or 3D Model (reachable from the + beside the Notes heading or the Add button in the note toolbar), the file types accepted (png, jpg, tif, bmp, gif, webp, svg, plus pdf — the official builds include PDF support, and it is only missing from a build compiled from source without Qt's PDF module, which Settings → PDF / SVG reports, and glb for LiDAR), one note per PDF page, PDF/SVG rasterization at the Settings resolution (96 ppi default), what import does to the file (copies the original into the project, applies EXIF orientation, caps very large images), and rotating (display only), removing, and revealing a note. There is no drag-and-drop.
- [Set the Image Resolution](notes/note-resolution.md): A note's DPI — why pixels need a real paper size before a scrap's "On Paper = In Cave" scale means anything, where the Image Info panel lives (Carpet mode only), measuring the resolution by clicking two points and giving their length on the paper, propagating one resolution to every note in the trip, resetting to the file's original, and what DPI does and doesn't change (it cancels out of an auto-calculated carpet but is load-bearing for a hand-typed scale). Mostly a raster concern: a PDF or SVG states its own page size so its resolution is computed, and a LiDAR note has no DPI at all. Fixes the "Invalid DPI" and "Invalid scale (check DPI)" errors.
- [Work with LiDAR Notes](notes/lidar-notes.md): Import a .glb scan as a note and georeference it. A scan does not replace sketching and won't out-draw a good sketcher. It records surfaces, and records them very well — better than anyone would draw a breakdown pile — but it cannot record what isn't a surface: airflow, which way on is worth pushing, where the water goes, or which features matter. A sketch is a set of judgements about exactly those things; a scan makes none. What a scan adds is a second dimension of survey data next to the sketch, useful for checking a sketch against the passage after the trip and for teaching a new sketcher. CaveWhere opens any polygonal glTF (.glb), not just LiDAR; PolyCam is the best-supported source and its scans arrive already up and already life-size, while photogrammetry models often come in with the wrong up and usually need their scale set by hand (photographs carry shape but not size). Order matters: set up first (+Y is up (PolyCam) is the default; Custom reveals an arrow tool), then north (auto-calculated from two or more stations, or set by tool/angle), then scale (normally 1:1; the row reads In Model, and a LiDAR note has no DPI). Place name-matched stations to tie the scan to the survey. LiDAR notes have no scraps, no leads, and no Rotate.
- [Scraps and Carpeting](scraps/carpeting.md): What a scrap is and how carpeting morphs a flat passage sketch onto the 3D survey (scale→grid→morph), why it re-drapes automatically when the survey changes, and the Compute Scraps / Scraps Visible debug controls.
- [Digitize a Scrap](scraps/digitize-a-scrap.md): Enter Carpet mode, trace a passage outline on a note by clicking points, place the survey stations inside it that anchor the scrap to the 3D survey, and mark leads (unexplored "go" passages) that morph onto the survey and collect on the cave's Leads page.
- [Choose the Scrap Type](scraps/scrap-types.md): The views surveyors draw (plan, projected profile, running profile, cross-section) illustrated from one passage, and how to pick the matching scrap type — plan (looking down), running profile (horizontal passage, wraps corners, keeps shot length), or projected profile (vertical shafts, one plane, foreshortens); cross-sections are drawn as projected profiles set by hand. Also the Scrap Info panel's other controls: Auto Calculate (derives values from stations and makes the rest read-only), north/up orientation, scale (On Paper = In Cave, computed from the note's DPI, with the 1:1 and unit-mismatch warnings), and the projected-profile-only azimuth.
- [Troubleshoot the Carpet](scraps/troubleshoot-carpeting.md): Why a carpet distorts and how to fix it — wrong scale (thin/fat) or rotation, mislabeled stations matched across the whole cave, wrong scrap type, plan vertical bumps, running-profile creases/seesaw, and splitting a sketch into smaller scraps.
- [Tune the Warping Settings](scraps/warping-settings.md): Adjust grid resolution (grid spacing over the scrap), shot interpolation spacing (dummy control points added along each shot), max closest stations (how many nearest stations warp each point — counted over the interpolated dummy stations too, so with the default 0.5 m spacing ten points reach about 5 m of survey line; the two settings multiply into that reach), and smoothing radius (Gaussian average of nearby heights; never applied to running profiles) to trade carpet detail against performance and smoothness; each has a diagram. These settings are global, not per-project.

## Meta

- [Authoring guide](AUTHORING.md): Conventions for writing manual pages — problem-first structure, front matter, links, and image rules. For contributors, not end users.
- [Manual index](index.md): Human-readable table of contents and chapter map.
